schools.summary: Chrysostom: there is nothing better, nothing more affectionate, than a spiritual teacher, who surpasses the kindness of any natural father; consider how this blessed one entreats the Philippians concerning the things to their advantage, and how earnestly, vehemently, and with much sympathy he speaks in exhorting them concerning concord, the cause of all good things; Paul beginning his plea for unity with a fourfold 'if' — any consolation in Christ, any comfort of love, any fellowship of the Spirit, any bowels and mercies — the heaping of motives to press for concord (Paul, with the warmth of a loving father, gathering up every tender argument to move the Philippians to unity: the encouragement they have in Christ, the comfort that flows from love, the fellowship shared in the Spirit, and the tender affections and compassions of the heart, as if to say, by all these things, be of one mind).

themes: if there be... any consolation in Christ
any comfort of love
any fellowship of the Spirit, any bowels and mercies
the fourfold appeal for unity

schools.summary: Gill: Paul's joy in them would be fulfilled (filled up, made complete) by their being of one mind, which is a great part of a minister's comfort in his people; Paul asking them to fulfil his joy by being likeminded, having the same love, being of one accord and one mind — the unity of mind and love that completes the apostle's joy (Paul making their concord the very thing that would crown his happiness, and describing it fully: a like-mindedness in judgment, a mutual and equal love, a harmony of spirit, 'one accord,' and a oneness of mind and aim, so the church's unity in thought and affection is set forth as the joy of its pastor).

themes: fulfil ye my joy, that ye be likeminded
having the same love, being of one accord, of one mind
the unity that completes the apostle's joy
the church's concord crowning its pastor

schools.summary: Gill: let nothing be done through strife (about words merely, mere contention to carry a point determined on without regard to reason and truth or yielding to the weak, the way of contentious persons) or vainglory (the empty desire of praise); Paul charging that nothing be done through strife or vainglory, but in lowliness of mind each esteem others better than themselves — the humility that roots out the seeds of division (Paul striking at the very roots of disunity, selfish contention and the craving for empty glory, and planting in their place the great grace of humility, by which each believer, lowly-minded, counts others above himself, not blind to others' faults but, conscious of his own, ready to honour and prefer them).

themes: let nothing be done through strife or vainglory
in lowliness of mind let each esteem other better than themselves
the humility that roots out division
striking at strife and vainglory

schools.summary: Gill: not but that a man should take care of his worldly affairs and look well to them and provide things honest in the sight of all men, for himself and his family (otherwise he would be worse than an infidel), but he is not to seek his own private advantage and prefer it to the public good (the Syriac reading 'neither let anyone be careful of himself, but of others also'); Paul charging that each look not only on his own things but also on the things of others — the unselfish regard for the good of others (Paul pressing the same humility into practical love: the believer not to be wrapped up in his own interests, gifts, and concerns alone, but to have a genuine, watchful care for the welfare, needs, and edification of his fellow-believers, seeking the common good and not merely his own).

themes: look not every man on his own things
but every man also on the things of others
the unselfish regard for the good of others
seeking the common good

schools.summary: Chrysostom: our Lord Jesus Christ, when exhorting his disciples to great actions, places before them himself, the Father, and the prophets as examples — 'for thus they did unto the prophets which were before you,' 'if they persecuted me, they will also persecute you,' 'learn of me, for I am meek,' 'be ye merciful' — so Paul here holds up Christ; Paul exhorting that the same mind be in them which was in Christ Jesus — Christ the supreme pattern of self-humbling love (Paul, to enforce the humility and unselfishness he has urged, pointing to the highest example of all: the mind, the disposition, of Christ Jesus himself, whose stupendous self-humbling, about to be described, is set before the believers as the model they are to copy, so the lowliness commanded is no mere counsel but a conformity to the very heart of their Lord).

themes: let this mind be in you
which was also in Christ Jesus
Christ the supreme pattern of self-humbling love
the lowliness a conformity to Christ's mind

schools.summary: Gill: 'being in the form of God' means the Father; Christ is the brightness of his glory and the express image of his person — this form understood not of any shape or figure (such is not to be seen of God), nor any accidental form (there being no accidents in God, whatever is in God being God), but his very nature and essence, for he is the Jehovah, I am what I am; Paul declaring that Christ, being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God — the true and proper deity of Christ (Paul laying the foundation of the great self-humbling in the height from which Christ stooped: he subsisted in the form of God, possessing the very nature, glory, and essence of Godhead, and counted his being equal with God no usurpation or robbery but his own rightful and natural possession, so the One who emptied himself was none other than the eternal, true God).

debates.question: What does 'in the form of God... thought it not robbery to be equal with God' teach?

sides.position: The true, eternal deity of Christ — he possessed the very nature and essence of Godhead and held equality with God as his own rightful, natural possession, not a usurpation

themes: who, being in the form of God
thought it not robbery to be equal with God
the true and proper deity of Christ
the height from which he stooped

schools.summary: Gill: 'made himself of no reputation' is literally 'emptied himself' — not of that fulness of grace laid up in him from everlasting (with which he appeared when made flesh), nor of the perfections of his divine nature (which were not in the least diminished by his assumption of human nature, for all the fulness of the Godhead dwelt in him bodily), but by taking the form of a servant and the likeness of men; Paul declaring that Christ emptied himself, taking the form of a servant and the likeness of men — the great self-emptying (kenosis) in the incarnation (Christ, who was in the form of God, emptying himself not by laying aside or diminishing his deity, which he could not, but by veiling its glory and taking to himself what he had not before: the 'form of a servant,' the real condition of a bond-servant, and 'the likeness of men,' a true and complete human nature, so the infinite God, without ceasing to be God, became a lowly man and servant).

sides.position: Not of his deity or divine perfections (which were not diminished, the fulness of the Godhead dwelling in him bodily) but by veiling its glory and taking what he had not — the form of a servant and a true human nature

themes: made himself of no reputation
took upon him the form of a servant
was made in the likeness of men
the great self-emptying (kenosis)

schools.summary: Gill: 'as a man' denotes not merely the likeness of a thing but the thing itself — he was really a man; the particle 'as' answering the Hebrew for likeness, which is sometimes likeness and sometimes truth and reality, the latter being the sense here; Paul declaring that Christ, found in fashion as a man, humbled himself and became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross — the lowest depth of the Saviour's self-humbling (Paul tracing the descent to its very bottom: Christ, truly man in his whole condition and appearance, not stopping at the incarnation but humbling himself yet further into obedience that went all the way to death, and not death merely but 'the death of the cross,' the most shameful, painful, and accursed death of all, so the humbling reached its uttermost in the cross).

themes: being found in fashion as a man, he humbled himself
became obedient unto death
even the death of the cross
the lowest depth of the Saviour's humbling

schools.summary: Gill: Paul proceeds to the exaltation of Christ for the encouragement of meek and humble souls — that whereas Christ, who so exceedingly demeaned himself, was afterwards highly exalted by God, so all who in imitation of him behave in lowliness of mind shall be exalted in God's due time, for whoso humbleth himself shall be exalted; Paul declaring that, wherefore, God highly exalted Christ and gave him a name above every name — the high reward of the great humbling (Paul turning from the descent to the ascent: because of his self-humbling, God 'highly exalted' Christ, raising him to the supreme height of glory and dominion in his resurrection, ascension, and session at God's right hand, and gave him 'a name above every name,' a dignity, authority, and renown surpassing all in heaven and earth, the depth of his humiliation answered by the height of his glory).

themes: wherefore God also hath highly exalted him
given him a name which is above every name
the high reward of the great humbling
the depth of humiliation answered by the height of glory

schools.summary: Gill: this is to be understood not of the outward act of bowing the knee upon hearing the syllables of the mere name Jesus pronounced (for in the bare name there is nothing to command such peculiar respect, it being a name common with the Jews, Joshua so called, and Barjesus), but of the homage and subjection due to the person and authority of Christ; Paul declaring that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, of things in heaven, earth, and under the earth — the universal submission due to the exalted Christ (the great end of Christ's exaltation being that to him, to 'the name of Jesus,' his person, dignity, and sovereign authority, all created beings throughout the universe, angels in heaven, men on earth, and the dead and the damned beneath, must bow in homage and subjection, the whole creation owning his lordship).

themes: that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow
of things in heaven, and things in earth, and things under the earth
the universal submission to the exalted Christ
the whole creation owning his lordship

schools.summary: Gill: confession is either true and hearty, when the mouth and heart agree in confessing (made only by true believers), or verbal only, in mere outward form and by force (as in hypocrites, wicked men, and devils themselves, who all either have confessed or will confess); Paul declaring that every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father — the universal acknowledgment of Christ's lordship to the Father's glory (with the bowing of every knee, every tongue shall also confess, own and acknowledge, that Jesus Christ is Lord, the willing loving confession of believers now and at the last the forced acknowledgment even of his enemies, and this universal owning of the Son redounding 'to the glory of God the Father,' whose purpose and whose Son are thereby honoured).

themes: every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord
to the glory of God the Father
the universal confession of Christ's lordship
willing in believers, forced in enemies

schools.summary: Chrysostom: admonitions should be accompanied with commendations, that they may become welcome, by referring those admonished to the zeal they have themselves exhibited; so Paul does not simply say 'be obedient' until he has first commended them, 'as ye have always obeyed'; Paul charging the beloved Philippians, who have always obeyed, now in his absence even more to work out their own salvation with fear and trembling — the diligent pursuit of salvation in reverent earnestness (Paul, commending their constant obedience, urging them to carry on their salvation to its full effect with diligent, active care, and that 'with fear and trembling,' not a slavish dread but a holy reverence and humble self-distrust, knowing how great the work and how real the danger of negligence).

themes: as ye have always obeyed... now much more in my absence
work out your own salvation
with fear and trembling
diligent pursuit in reverent earnestness

schools.summary: Gill: this is both an encouragement to persons conscious of their own weakness to work, as exhorted, and an argument for humility and meekness against pride and vainglory, since all we have and do is from God; it points out the spring, principle, and foundation of all good works, namely the grace of God wrought in the heart; Paul giving the ground of the foregoing exhortation — that it is God who works in them both to will and to do of his good pleasure — the divine working that grounds the human working (Paul, far from making the believer's effort the rival of grace, making it rest upon grace: the very reason they can and must work out their salvation is that God himself is at work within them, producing both the will, the inclination and desire, and the deed, the performance, and that according to his own good pleasure, so the whole of the believer's good is the gift and effect of God's gracious working).

debates.question: How do 'work out your own salvation' (v.12) and 'God worketh in you' (v.13) relate?

sides.position: The human working rests on and flows from the divine — the believer can and must work precisely because God works in him both the will and the deed; grace grounds effort, not rivals it

themes: for it is God which worketh in you
both to will and to do
of his good pleasure
the divine working that grounds the human

schools.summary: Gill: 'do all things' means not evil things (these are to be abhorred, shunned, and avoided, even all appearance of them), nor all indifferent things at all times and under all circumstances (when the peace and edification of others are in danger), but all good things agreeable to the righteous law and good will of God; Paul charging them to do all things without murmurings and disputings — the cheerful, peaceable doing of duty (Paul, having urged the diligent working out of salvation, adding the manner: all the good they do to be done without 'murmurings,' the secret grumbling and discontent against God or man that poisons obedience, and without 'disputings,' the contentious wranglings and doubtful reasonings that breed strife, so their service is to be both contented and peaceable, free of complaint within and quarrel without).

themes: do all things
without murmurings and disputings
the cheerful, peaceable doing of duty
free of complaint within and quarrel without

schools.summary: Gill: this respects not their being blameless in the sight of God (the saints are not so in themselves, being not without sin, though they are as considered in Christ, clothed with his righteousness and washed in his blood), but before men — being blameless in their conduct, that the enemy may have nothing evil to say of them; Paul stating the aim — that they may be blameless, harmless, the sons of God without rebuke, in the midst of a crooked and perverse nation, shining as lights in the world — the holy, shining witness in a dark world (Paul setting the goal of the peaceable obedience: that the Philippians may be blameless, giving no just cause of reproach, and harmless, pure and innocent, unrebukable children of God, and that precisely 'in the midst of a crooked and perverse nation,' where, like lights set in a dark place, they shine by their holy lives, displaying the grace of God before a fallen world).

themes: that ye may be blameless and harmless, the sons of God
in the midst of a crooked and perverse nation
ye shine as lights in the world
the holy, shining witness in a dark world

schools.summary: Gill: the 'word of life' may mean either Christ the essential Word, in whom life was and is (the quick or living Word, having all life in himself as God and as Mediator for his people), or the gospel, which is the word that gives and shows the way of life; Paul speaking of them holding forth the word of life, that he might rejoice in the day of Christ that he had not run or laboured in vain — the witness of the gospel and the minister's joy in his converts (Paul calling the Philippians to hold forth, display and offer to others as a lamp holds forth its light, the word of life, the gospel, so their steadfast and shining profession might be, in the day of Christ, the proof that his apostolic running and toil among them had not been fruitless but had borne the lasting fruit that would be his joy and crown).

themes: holding forth the word of life
that I may rejoice in the day of Christ
that I have not run in vain, neither laboured in vain
the believers' profession the minister's joy and crown

schools.summary: Gill: 'if I be offered' is literally 'poured out,' as the drink offerings of wine or oil were poured upon the sacrifice; meaning the effusion of his blood, which he compares to a libation, and the laying down of his life for the sake of Christ and his gospel, which he knew not how soon might be; Paul declaring that, even if he be poured out as a drink offering upon the sacrifice and service of their faith, he joys and rejoices with them all — the apostle's joyful readiness to die for their faith (Paul, taking up the imagery of sacrifice, regarding their faith and the offering of themselves to God as the sacrifice, and his own blood, should he be martyred, as the drink-offering poured out upon it, and far from shrinking welcoming such a death with joy, rejoicing that his life should be spent and even given for the confirmation of their faith).

themes: if I be offered upon the sacrifice and service of your faith
I joy, and rejoice with you all
poured out as a libation for their faith
welcoming martyrdom with joy

schools.summary: Gill: Paul would not have them be sorrowful should they hear of his death for the sake of the gospel and of his blood being poured out in such a cause, since it was as a libation on their faith and for the confirmation of it, and would be gain to Christ and his interest, and to the apostle also; therefore they should be so far from grieving as to rejoice; Paul calling them, for the same cause, to joy and rejoice with him — the shared joy that triumphs over the prospect of death (Paul, having voiced his own joy at the prospect of being poured out for them, turning and bidding them rejoice with him for the same reason, so that, far from grieving over his martyrdom as a calamity, they should share his gladness, counting his death for the gospel a thing of joy not sorrow, gain to Christ, to themselves, and to Paul).

themes: for the same cause also do ye joy
and rejoice with me
the shared joy over Paul's death
counting his martyrdom gain, not calamity

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul had said his bonds had fallen out to the progress of the gospel (Philippians 1:12-13) and that he might be poured out upon the sacrifice of their faith (2:17); by these words he strengthened them, and lest they suspect his former words were spoken just to comfort them, he now shows his continued care; Paul trusting in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy shortly, that he might be comforted by knowing their state — the apostle's loving concern to hear of the church (Paul, even amid his own uncertain imprisonment, so concerned for the Philippians that he plans to send Timothy to them, not for his own ease but that he himself might be cheered by receiving sure news of their spiritual welfare, his comfort bound up with their well-being, and all his planning submitted to the Lord's will, 'I trust in the Lord Jesus').

themes: I trust in the Lord Jesus to send Timotheus shortly
that I also may be of good comfort, when I know your state
the apostle's loving concern for the church
his comfort bound up with their welfare

schools.summary: Gill: Timothy had a soul like the apostle's (the Syriac rendering 'as my soul'), which none that were with him besides had; he was of the same judgment with Paul in the doctrines of grace, received and preached the same gospel, preached the same Christ the Son of God without yea and nay, and had the same affection; Paul saying he has no man likeminded with himself, who would naturally (genuinely) care for the Philippians' state — the rare and kindred spirit of the true pastor (Paul commending Timothy as one of a kind among his companions: a man so like Paul in mind and heart that he could be trusted to care for the Philippians with a genuine, almost instinctive concern, not from self-interest but from real love for the church, a true pastor's heart such as Paul found in no other then with him).

themes: I have no man likeminded
who will naturally care for your state
Timothy's singular kindred spirit
a true pastor's genuine care

schools.summary: Gill: Paul means not every individual but the greatest part, and not merely such as were manifestly false teachers but such as were with him as ministers of the word, he being stripped, by one means or another, of the more valuable preachers of the gospel and in much such a case as he describes himself elsewhere; Paul lamenting that all (the most part) seek their own things, not the things which are Jesus Christ's — the rebuke of self-seeking even among ministers (Paul, explaining why Timothy stood so alone, sadly observing that the generality even of those about him sought their own interests, ease, safety, profit, reputation, rather than the cause of Christ, so few could be found willing to undertake a hard and self-denying errand for the church's good).

themes: for all seek their own
not the things which are Jesus Christ's
the rebuke of self-seeking even among ministers
explaining why Timothy stood alone

schools.summary: Gill: the Philippians had had an experiment of Timothy, a trial of his spirit and a proof of his gifts and ministry, when he was among them with Paul at his first preaching the gospel there (to the conversion of Lydia, the jailer, and their households, laying the foundation of a gospel church state); Paul reminding them that they know the proof of Timothy, who served with Paul in the gospel as a son with a father — the tested fidelity and devoted service of Timothy (Paul appealing to the Philippians' own knowledge: they had themselves seen the proof of Timothy when he laboured among them, and knew how he had served alongside Paul in the work of the gospel, not as a hireling but with the loving submission and devotion of a son toward a father, sharing the apostle's toils as a true child shares a father's labour).

themes: ye know the proof of him
as a son with the father, he hath served with me in the gospel
Timothy's tested fidelity
devoted, filial service

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul was a prisoner at Rome, and there was not a little uncertainty whether he would be condemned or acquitted (he was, it is commonly supposed, in fact released on the first trial, 2 Timothy 4:16), and he now felt he would soon be able to send Timothy to them; Paul stating that he hopes to send Timothy presently, as soon as he sees how his own case will go — the plan held in submission to the outcome of his trial (Paul, eager to send so trusted a messenger, yet waiting to know the issue of his imprisonment, whether he is to be freed or condemned, keeping Timothy by him a little longer until that be settled, so even his loving plans for the church bend to the providence governing his own uncertain fate).

themes: him therefore I hope to send presently
so soon as I shall see how it will go with me
the plan held in submission to his trial
his plans bending to providence

schools.summary: Ellicott: compare Philemon 1:22, 'prepare me a lodging, for I trust that through your prayers I shall be given to you,' where the expectation seems even more immediate (the interval between the letters unknown); the received belief is that Paul was released and visited the East again; Paul trusting in the Lord that he himself shall also come shortly — the apostle's own hopeful expectation of release and reunion (Paul, not content to send Timothy only, expressing his confidence, resting 'in the Lord,' submitted to his will, that he himself will be set free and come to the Philippians before long, his loving desire to see them again joined to a believing hope grounded not in his own reckoning but in the Lord's gracious providence).

themes: I trust in the Lord
that I also myself shall come shortly
the apostle's hopeful expectation of reunion
grounded in the Lord's providence

schools.summary: Gill: Epaphroditus was sent by the Philippians to the apostle with a present, and had been detained at Rome for some time, partly through business and partly through sickness; now recovered, Paul thought it proper to send him back; Paul declaring it necessary to send Epaphroditus — his brother, companion in labour, fellowsoldier, the Philippians' messenger and minister to his wants — the warm honour paid to a faithful fellow-worker (Paul, sending back the man the Philippians had sent to him, heaping on him a string of honourable titles that show his worth and the bonds between them: a brother in the faith, a sharer in Paul's gospel labour, a fellowsoldier in the spiritual warfare, and the church's own messenger who had ministered to Paul's needs, so Epaphroditus is commended as one worthy of all esteem).

themes: I supposed it necessary to send to you Epaphroditus
my brother, and companion in labour, and fellowsoldier
your messenger, and he that ministered to my wants
the warm commendation of a faithful fellow-worker

schools.summary: Gill: this verse and the next contain the reasons of Paul's sending him; the first is that Epaphroditus had a vehement and longing desire after all of them, to see them (the Alexandrian and other copies adding 'to see you'); and he was full of heaviness, not so much for his own sickness as because they had heard of it and were grieved; Paul giving the reason for sending him — that Epaphroditus longed after them all and was full of heaviness because they had heard of his sickness — the tender mutual love between the messenger and the church (Paul showing the affectionate heart of Epaphroditus, who, far from his fellow-believers and ill, was filled with homesick longing for them, and whose chief distress was not his own illness but the sorrow he knew it had caused them when the news reached them, his grief a grief of love, wishing to relieve their anxiety).

themes: for he longed after you all
was full of heaviness
because that ye had heard that he had been sick
his chief distress the sorrow it caused them

schools.summary: Gill: it was not a mere rumour or false alarm but real matter of fact, and no light disorder or slight indisposition but a very dangerous illness — though the sickness was not unto death, yet near it; good men, such as Christ loves (as he did Lazarus), are sometimes sick, though their spiritual diseases are healed and their sins forgiven; Paul declaring that Epaphroditus was indeed sick nigh unto death, but God had mercy on him, and on Paul also, sparing him sorrow upon sorrow — the mercy of God in sparing both the sick man and the apostle (Paul confirming the gravity of the illness, truly at death's door, and tracing the recovery to God's mercy alone, a mercy shown not only to Epaphroditus in restoring his life but to Paul himself, for had Epaphroditus died, the apostle, already burdened with his imprisonment, would have had 'sorrow upon sorrow,' grief heaped on grief, from which God's mercy spared him).

themes: for indeed he was sick nigh unto death
but God had mercy on him; and not on him only, but on me also
lest I should have sorrow upon sorrow
God's mercy in sparing both

schools.summary: Gill: 'the more carefully' means in greater haste, as soon as possible; Barnes: with more diligence or speed, Paul being the more ready to send him, that on seeing him their minds might be at ease and they might rejoice; Paul stating that he sent Epaphroditus the more speedily, that they might rejoice on seeing him again, and that Paul himself might be less sorrowful — the speeding of the messenger for the comfort of all (Paul, in his unselfish care, hurrying Epaphroditus home so that the Philippians, grieved by news of his sickness, might have the joy of seeing him restored and well, and that Paul's own burden of concern, for them and for their grief, might be eased, his sorrow lightened by the knowledge of their gladness).

themes: I sent him therefore the more carefully
that, when ye see him again, ye may rejoice
that I may be the less sorrowful
the messenger sped home for mutual comfort

schools.summary: Gill: they were to receive him not only into their houses (where such as bring the doctrine of Christ should be admitted) but into their hearts and affections, into fellowship and communion, and to the exercise of his office among them as their minister; Paul charging them to receive Epaphroditus in the Lord with all gladness, and to hold such men in reputation — the warm reception and honour due to faithful servants of Christ (Paul bidding the Philippians welcome their returning messenger heartily and joyfully, 'in the Lord,' as a Christian brother and minister, and laying down a general rule beyond this one case: that such faithful, self-denying servants of Christ are to be held in honour and esteem by the church, their worth acknowledged and their persons valued).

themes: receive him therefore in the Lord with all gladness
hold such in reputation
the honour due to faithful servants of Christ
worth acknowledged, persons valued

schools.summary: Gill: 'the work of Christ' is that the church sent him about, which he cheerfully undertook and faithfully performed in carrying the present to and visiting the apostle in prison, called the work of Christ because taken by Christ as if done to himself (what is done to his servants); and Epaphroditus, what with the long and fatiguing journey from Philippi and other hardships, risked his life; Paul giving the ground of the honour due him — that for the work of Christ Epaphroditus came near death, not regarding his life, to supply what was lacking in their service to Paul — the noble self-forgetfulness that risked life for Christ's work (Epaphroditus having brought himself to death's door for the work of Christ, recklessly hazarding, 'not regarding,' his own life to do that ministry to the imprisoned apostle which the distant Philippians could not render in person, so his near-fatal illness was the badge of a love that counted its own life cheap for the gospel's sake).

themes: for the work of Christ he was nigh unto death
not regarding his life
to supply your lack of service toward me
the self-sacrificing devotion of Epaphroditus
